news_summary: Advancements in welding techniques, discoveries in quantum physics, innovative archaeological insights, and thoughtful integration of AI in academia highlight today's exciting progress across technology and science.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées dans les techniques de soudage, les découvertes en physique quantique, les percées archéologiques novatrices et l'intégration réfléchie de l'intelligence artificielle dans le monde universitaire illustrent les progrès passionnants réalisés aujourd'hui dans les domaines de la technologie et de la science.
news_summary_es: Los avances en las técnicas de soldadura, los descubrimientos en física cuántica, los nuevos hallazgos arqueológicos y la integración reflexiva de la inteligencia artificial en el ámbito académico ponen de relieve los apasionantes avances actuales en tecnología y ciencia.

articles.title: A Combination of Techniques Leads to Improved Friction Stir Welding
articles.summary: Download PDF: A Combination of Techniques Leads to Improved Friction Stir Welding The NESC developed several innovative tools and techniques during an assessment to find the root cause of poor tensile strength and low topography anomalies (LTA) in welds formed using a solid-state welding process called self-reacting friction stir welding (SRFSW).    Using a combination of […]
articles.content:
Download PDF: A Combination of Techniques Leads to Improved Friction Stir Welding

The NESC developed several innovative tools and techniques during an assessment to find the root cause of poor tensile strength and low topography anomalies (LTA) in welds formed using a solid-state welding process called self-reacting friction stir welding (SRFSW).

Using a combination of machine learning, statistical modeling, and physics-based simulations, the assessment team helped improve the weld process and solve both issues, lifting constraints that had been placed on flight hardware.

Developing Techniques for LTA Detection

Determining the root cause of poor tensile strength welds and LTA observed on the weld fracture surfaces involved several techniques:

Deep Learning for LTA Detection: The NESC team developed a machine-learning model to detect and segment LTA in weld images. The model was trained on images annotated by metallurgy experts, with a majority-vote consensus to resolve disagreements. The team then developed an accompanying standard operating procedure for image capture to improve robustness and reduce bias. This model was built on previous NASA work to develop specialty microscopy analysis foundation models by pretraining on 100,000+ microscopy images. This step was crucial to linking process parameters with LTA occurrence in an objective, nonbiased way.

The team eliminated issues with manual identification of LTA by training a neural network to detect LTA from images of fracture surfaces, pretraining an encoder on a large NASA dataset of microscopy images called MicroNet.

Integrated Data-Ingestion Framework: SRFSW is a complex process with many interacting variables. The weld process produces a large amount of data with diverse data types that include dozens of tabular process parameters, dozens of sequential data streams from the production tool, fracture and weld cross-section images, and mechanical-test lab data. A Python-based framework was developed to automatically ingest and validate these diverse data and compile them into a single master spreadsheet and a database. This tool reduced manual effort, minimized transcription errors, and improved data quality for downstream analysis. The team delivered the tool to stakeholders for their ongoing use.

Data Analysis Web Application: A new web-based visualization and analysis tool allowed engineers and subject matter experts to quickly explore the integrated dataset for faster hypothesis testing and more intuitive insight generation throughout the investigation

Space-Filling Design of Experiments: Because SRFSW involves complex, nonlinear relationships between process parameters, the team found traditional factorial designs were insufficient and implemented a space-filling design of experiments (DOE) to efficiently explore the full parameter space. These data-trained machine-learning models capture the underlying weld behavior. The team also developed a software tool for generating such designs and shared it with stakeholders.

Physics-Based SRFSW Simulation: Creating a computational model of the SRFSW process simulated weld conditions, microstructure evolution, and resulting properties, offering insight into aspects of the weld process that are inaccessible to physical sensors. This enhanced understanding and guided improvements.

Determining LTA Root Cause

Using these tools and analyses, the team identified two root causes for the LTA and poor tensile strength:

Overly aggressive post-weld surface preparation in production reduced weld strength.

Weld power input outside the optimal range led to inconsistent welds and increased risk of LTA.

The process models helped define a target weld power input window and recommended how to adjust primary control parameters to reliably achieve that target. Follow-up production tests confirmed that these adjustments could be implemented with high precision, eliminating both low-strength welds and LTA.

Friction Stir Welding

In SRFSW, a rotating pin is plunged into the seam between two metal plates, generating heat through friction that fuses the sheets together without melting the material. This technique produces stronger joints than traditional welding and enables the use of high-performance but traditionally non-weldable alloys like Aluminum 2219.

The SRFSW technique uses no blowtorches or solder because friction stirs the materials together at a molecular level.

NASA’s Friction Stir Welding lab resides inside NASA’s Michoud Vertical Assembly Center in New Orleans and is being used to join major components of the SLS rocket.

For information, contact Donald S. Parker. donald.s.parker@nasa.gov

References: NASA/TM-20240016466 and NASA/TM-20230010624
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant technological advancement in friction stir welding by combining machine learning, physics-based simulations, and innovative data tools to solve key quality issues. This breakthrough improves the reliability and strength of welds used in critical aerospace hardware, benefiting large-scale space exploration efforts and potentially other industries. The story has broad significance, clear impact, and detailed context on the methods and results.
articles.personality_title: NASA improves friction stir welding with new machine learning and simulation tools
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA uses a special welding method called self-reacting friction stir welding (SRFSW) to join metal parts for rockets. This method creates strong joints without melting the metal, but recent welds showed problems like weak spots and surface flaws.

**What happened** – A team at NASA’s Engineering and Safety Center (NESC) combined machine learning, computer simulations, and new data tools to find why some welds were weak or had defects called low topography anomalies (LTA). They trained a smart computer program to spot these defects in images, created software to gather and analyze all welding data automatically, and built detailed models to simulate the welding process.

**Impact** – These tools helped the team discover that too rough surface cleaning after welding and incorrect power settings caused the weak welds and flaws. By adjusting the welding power and cleaning steps, they removed these problems, making welds stronger and more reliable. This is important because these welds are used in building NASA’s Space Launch System rockets, where safety and strength are critical.

**What’s next step** – NASA will use these new techniques to keep improving welding for future rocket parts. The data tools and models will help engineers quickly find and fix issues in production. This approach might also be shared with other industries that rely on strong metal joints.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA combined smart computer programs and simulations to fix welding flaws, making rocket parts stronger and safer.
articles.personality_title_fr: La NASA améliore le soudage par friction malaxage grâce à l’apprentissage automatique et aux simulations
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La NASA utilise une méthode spéciale de soudage appelée soudage par friction malaxage auto-réactif (SRFSW) pour assembler des pièces métalliques destinées aux fusées. Cette méthode crée des joints solides sans faire fondre le métal, mais certains soudures récentes présentaient des faiblesses et des défauts de surface.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe du Centre d’ingénierie et de sécurité de la NASA (NESC) a combiné apprentissage automatique, simulations informatiques et nouveaux outils de données pour comprendre pourquoi certaines soudures étaient faibles ou avaient des anomalies appelées défauts de topographie basse (LTA). Ils ont entraîné un programme intelligent pour détecter ces défauts sur des images, créé des logiciels pour collecter et analyser automatiquement toutes les données de soudage, et construit des modèles détaillés pour simuler le processus de soudage.

**Impact** – Ces outils ont permis à l’équipe de découvrir que trop de nettoyage agressif après le soudage et des réglages de puissance incorrects étaient à l’origine des soudures faibles et des défauts. En ajustant la puissance de soudage et les étapes de nettoyage, ils ont éliminé ces problèmes, rendant les soudures plus solides et fiables. Ceci est important car ces soudures sont utilisées pour construire les fusées du système de lancement spatial de la NASA, où la sécurité et la solidité sont essentielles.

**Prochaine étape** – La NASA utilisera ces nouvelles techniques pour continuer à améliorer le soudage des futures pièces de fusée. Les outils de données et les modèles aideront les ingénieurs à détecter et corriger rapidement les problèmes en production. Cette méthode pourrait aussi être partagée avec d’autres industries qui ont besoin de joints métalliques solides.

**En une phrase** – La NASA a combiné des programmes informatiques intelligents et des simulations pour corriger les défauts de soudure, rendant les pièces de fusée plus solides et sûres.
articles.personality_title_es: La NASA mejora la soldadura por fricción con aprendizaje automático y simulaciones
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La NASA usa un método especial llamado soldadura por fricción auto-reactiva (SRFSW) para unir piezas metálicas de cohetes. Este método crea uniones fuertes sin fundir el metal, pero algunas soldaduras recientes mostraron puntos débiles y defectos en la superficie.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo del Centro de Ingeniería y Seguridad de la NASA (NESC) combinó aprendizaje automático, simulaciones por computadora y nuevas herramientas de datos para descubrir por qué algunas soldaduras eran débiles o tenían anomalías llamadas anomalías de baja topografía (LTA). Entrenaron un programa inteligente para detectar estos defectos en imágenes, crearon software para recopilar y analizar automáticamente todos los datos de la soldadura, y construyeron modelos detallados para simular el proceso de soldadura.

**Impacto** – Estas herramientas ayudaron al equipo a descubrir que una limpieza demasiado agresiva después de la soldadura y ajustes incorrectos de potencia causaban las soldaduras débiles y defectos. Ajustando la potencia de soldadura y los pasos de limpieza, eliminaron estos problemas, haciendo las soldaduras más fuertes y confiables. Esto es importante porque estas soldaduras se usan para construir los cohetes del Sistema de Lanzamiento Espacial de la NASA, donde la seguridad y la fuerza son vitales.

**Próximo paso** – La NASA usará estas nuevas técnicas para seguir mejorando la soldadura de piezas futuras de cohetes. Las herramientas de datos y los modelos ayudarán a los ingenieros a detectar y corregir problemas rápidamente en la producción. Este enfoque también podría compartirse con otras industrias que dependen de uniones metálicas fuertes.

**Resumen en una frase** – La NASA combinó programas inteligentes y simulaciones para corregir defectos de soldadura, haciendo las piezas de cohetes más fuertes y seguras.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of two interlocking metal plates being gently fused together by a glowing, swirling pin of energy at their seam, surrounded by abstract representations of data streams and neural network patterns softly illuminating the background, symbolizing the fusion of advanced machine learning, physics simulation, and precision engineering in friction stir welding.

articles.title: A strange new quantum state appears when atoms get “frustrated”
articles.summary: Physicists at UC Santa Barbara have uncovered a new way to manipulate unusual magnetic states by exploiting “frustration” inside a crystal’s atomic structure. The team discovered a rare system where two different kinds of frustration—magnetic and electronic bond frustration—coexist and interact. By coupling these competing effects, researchers may be able to control exotic quantum states, potentially unlocking new ways to manipulate entangled spins for future quantum technologies.
articles.content:
In the laboratory of UC Santa Barbara materials scientist Stephen Wilson, researchers are investigating the physics behind unusual states of matter while designing materials that could support properties useful for future quantum technologies.

In research published in Nature Materials, Wilson's team describes a new way to use a phenomenon known as frustration of long range order in a material to create unconventional magnetic states. These states may eventually have relevance for quantum technologies. Wilson emphasized that the work focuses on basic science rather than immediate applications. "This is fundamental science aimed at addressing a basic question. It's meant to probe what physics may be possible for future devices."

Their study, titled "Interleaved bond frustration in a triangular lattice antiferromagnet," examines how multiple forms of frustration can arise in these systems. One important type is geometric frustration. This occurs when magnetic moments in a material cannot settle into a single stable pattern and instead remain in a fluctuating configuration.

Tiny Atomic Magnets and Frustrated Geometry

Wilson explained magnetism using a simple analogy. "You can think of magnetism as being derived from tiny bar magnets sitting at the atomic sites in a crystal lattice," he said. These tiny magnets are called magnetic dipole moments. Depending on the structure of a material, they interact with one another and arrange themselves in ways that minimize their energy or, in other words, reach their ground state. The ground state represents the lowest possible energy configuration of a system, and at absolute zero temperature every system exists in this state.

Wilson continued, "If those magnetic moments interact in a way that wants them to point antiparallel to one another, we call that antiferromagnetism." In a square arrangement of atoms, this interaction works easily. Each magnetic moment can point opposite to its neighbors, producing a stable configuration.

However, things change when the atoms form a triangular arrangement. In that geometry, it becomes impossible for every magnetic moment to point opposite to all of its neighbors at the same time. As Wilson described it, the moments begin competing with each other. They are effectively frustrated because the geometry of the lattice prevents them from achieving the lowest energy arrangement. The system tries to reach equilibrium but cannot fully do so because of the structure it occupies.

Bond Frustration and Electron Sharing

A similar type of frustration can occur in another aspect of electrons. Instead of involving magnetism, it can arise from electron charge. When two nearby ions attempt to share an electron across a bond, they may form what scientists call an atomic dimer.

Just as magnetic interactions can be frustrated in certain lattice structures, these dimers can also face restrictions in geometries such as triangular lattices or honeycomb networks. The result can be a network of bonds that is itself frustrated. Such a network is often very sensitive to strain, and applying strain can partially relieve the frustration within the bonding pattern.

Wilson's study focuses on an extremely rare class of materials where both types of frustration exist at the same time. Magnetic frustration and bond frustration appear together in the same structure.

Coupling Two Frustrated Systems

Wilson described the finding as "exciting" because it opens a possible route to controlling one frustrated system by influencing the other. Over the past six or seven years, scientists have learned how to create frustrated magnetic states using materials built from triangular networks of lanthanides, a group of elements found along the bottom row of the periodic table.

"In principle, this triangular lattice network of properly chosen lanthanide moments can cause a special kind of intrinsically quantum disordered state to arise," Wilson said. The team's goal was to build on that idea. "One thing we tried to do in this project was to functionalize that exotic state by embedding it in a crystal lattice that has an additional degree of bond frustration."

Researchers know that quantum disordered magnetism can take several forms. Some of these states may support long range entanglement among spins, which is a key concept in quantum information science. Wilson explained, "Some states can host long-range entanglement of spins, which is of interest in the realm of quantum information. Gaining control over those states via applying a strain in the frustrated bond network would be exciting."

Toward Controlling Quantum States

When two frustrated systems exist together and are both highly sensitive to disturbances such as strain or magnetic fields, an important question emerges. Scientists want to know whether the two systems can influence each other. If one layer becomes ordered under certain conditions, it could potentially affect the other layer as well.

"It's a way of imparting in things a functionality or response to other things to which it would otherwise not respond," Wilson explained. "So, in principle, one can engineer large ferroic responses.You can apply a bit of strain, which induces magnetic order, or you can apply a bit of magnetic field and induce changes to the structure.

"Again, in principle, if you can find a quantum disordered ground state that hosts long-range entanglement, the question then becomes whether you can access that entanglement by, for instance, coupling to another layer, such as bond frustration."

Wilson is also interested in whether this approach could lead to multiple types of order emerging together. "Basically, you could have different types of order that get nucleated because of the proximity of these two frustrated lattices," he said. "That's the big-picture idea."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery in quantum materials physics, revealing a new way to manipulate exotic quantum states by coupling two types of atomic-scale frustration. This fundamental research has broad implications for future quantum technologies, potentially enabling new methods to control entangled spins, which is crucial for quantum information science. The study is detailed and focused on a breakthrough with meaningful potential impact beyond a niche audience.
articles.personality_title: Scientists discover new way to control unusual quantum states using atomic frustration
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists study how atoms and their tiny magnetic forces behave in materials, especially when these forces cannot settle into simple patterns because of the material’s shape. This is called frustration and can create unusual magnetic states.

**What happened** – Researchers at UC Santa Barbara found a rare material where two kinds of frustration happen together: one involving magnetic forces and another involving how electrons share bonds between atoms. These two frustrations interact in a way that might help scientists control strange quantum states.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because controlling these frustrated states could help manage entangled spins, which are tiny magnetic properties linked over distance and key to quantum technologies like super-powerful computers. The work shows a new path to influence quantum states by using the material’s structure.

**What's next step** – Scientists will explore how applying strain or magnetic fields can change one frustrated system and affect the other. This could lead to creating new materials with multiple types of order working together, advancing quantum device design.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Researchers found a new way to link two atomic frustrations in materials, opening possibilities to control complex quantum states for future technology.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques découvrent une nouvelle façon de contrôler des états quantiques inhabituels grâce à la frustration atomique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient comment les atomes et leurs minuscules forces magnétiques se comportent dans les matériaux, surtout lorsque ces forces ne peuvent pas se stabiliser à cause de la forme du matériau. Cela s'appelle la frustration et peut créer des états magnétiques inhabituels.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de l'Université de Californie à Santa Barbara ont trouvé un matériau rare où deux types de frustration se produisent en même temps : une liée aux forces magnétiques et une autre à la façon dont les électrons partagent des liaisons entre atomes. Ces deux frustrations interagissent d'une manière qui pourrait aider à contrôler des états quantiques étranges.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car contrôler ces états frustrés pourrait aider à gérer les spins intriqués, de minuscules propriétés magnétiques liées à distance et essentielles pour les technologies quantiques comme les ordinateurs très puissants. Ce travail montre une nouvelle façon d'influencer les états quantiques grâce à la structure du matériau.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont explorer comment appliquer une contrainte ou des champs magnétiques peut changer un système frustré et affecter l'autre. Cela pourrait mener à créer de nouveaux matériaux avec plusieurs types d'ordre travaillant ensemble, faisant avancer la conception des dispositifs quantiques.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les chercheurs ont trouvé un nouveau moyen de relier deux frustrations atomiques dans les matériaux, ouvrant des possibilités pour contrôler des états quantiques complexes pour la technologie future.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren nueva forma de controlar estados cuánticos inusuales usando frustración atómica
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos estudian cómo se comportan los átomos y sus pequeñas fuerzas magnéticas en los materiales, especialmente cuando estas fuerzas no pueden establecer patrones simples debido a la forma del material. Esto se llama frustración y puede crear estados magnéticos inusuales.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de California en Santa Bárbara encontraron un material raro donde ocurren dos tipos de frustración al mismo tiempo: una relacionada con fuerzas magnéticas y otra con cómo los electrones comparten enlaces entre átomos. Estas dos frustraciones interactúan de manera que podrían ayudar a controlar estados cuánticos extraños.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque controlar estos estados frustrados podría ayudar a manejar espines entrelazados, que son pequeñas propiedades magnéticas conectadas a distancia y clave para tecnologías cuánticas como computadoras muy potentes. El trabajo muestra una nueva manera de influir en estados cuánticos usando la estructura del material.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos explorarán cómo aplicar tensión o campos magnéticos puede cambiar un sistema frustrado y afectar al otro. Esto podría llevar a crear nuevos materiales con varios tipos de orden trabajando juntos, avanzando en el diseño de dispositivos cuánticos.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Los investigadores encontraron una nueva forma de unir dos frustraciones atómicas en materiales, abriendo posibilidades para controlar estados cuánticos complejos para tecnología futura.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of an intricate triangular lattice made of softly glowing atomic magnets represented as tiny, gently spinning bar magnets with delicate arrows, interwoven with shimmering, flexible bonds symbolizing electron-sharing dimers, both subtly strained and interacting, set against a calm, abstract background that evokes a sense of quantum entanglement and delicate balance.

articles.title: The smell of Egyptian mummies is revealing 2,000-year-old secrets
articles.summary: The distinctive smell of ancient mummies is helping scientists decode the secrets of Egyptian mummification. By analyzing tiny traces of chemicals in the air around mummy samples, researchers identified dozens of compounds linked to oils, resins, beeswax, and bitumen used during embalming. The chemical clues reveal that mummification grew increasingly complex over thousands of years.
articles.content:
Mummification has fascinated historians and scientists for centuries, yet many details about how ancient Egyptians preserved the dead have remained unclear. New research now shows that the distinctive musty scent of mummified remains holds valuable clues about how these elaborate burial rituals were carried out.

A study led by chemists at the University of Bristol found that the smell associated with mummies is not simply the result of aging or decay. Instead, it reflects a complex blend of substances used during embalming, along with preserved fabrics and materials wrapped around the body. Together, these lingering chemical traces reveal how mummification methods changed and became more sophisticated over hundreds of years.

Lead author Dr. Wanyue Zhao, Research Associate in Organic Geochemistry at the University of Bristol, said: "The findings mark a significant step forward in improving our understanding of Egyptian history and the fascinating ritual of mummification. Our analysis of the associated scents has uncovered new insights into how the practice developed through the ages and became increasingly sophisticated."

Analyzing the Air Around Ancient Remains

To investigate the source of mummy scents, researchers examined the air surrounding extremely small mummy fragments about the size of a peppercorn. This approach differs from traditional techniques, which often require dissolving samples in solvents and can damage delicate artifacts.

The team used a combination of advanced analytical tools including solid phase microextraction, gas chromatography, and high resolution mass spectrometry. These methods allowed them to capture gases inside small sealed containers and separate the different scent components known as Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs) so they could be studied in detail.

The research, published in the Journal of Archaeological Science, analyzed 35 samples of balms and bandages from 19 mummies. These remains spanned more than 2,000 years of Egyptian history between 3200 BC and 395 AD. Across all samples, scientists identified 81 distinct VOCs that offer clues about the materials used during the embalming process and the time periods in which the mummies were prepared.

Chemical Clues Reveal Embalming Ingredients

Even when present in extremely small quantities, these chemical compounds helped researchers identify the substances used in preservation. They grouped the compounds into four main categories connected to specific embalming ingredients.

Fats and oils generated aromatic compounds and short chain fatty acids. Beeswax produced mono-carboxylic fatty acids and cinnamic compounds. Plant resins released aromatic compounds and sesquiterpenoids, while bitumen produced naphthenic compounds.

Dr. Zhao said: "Our findings showed the chemical patterns varied across historical periods. Earlier mummies had simpler profiles dominated by fats and oils, while later mummies displayed more complex mixtures incorporating imported resins and bitumen. Such materials were more costly and required more specialized preparation, as the practice became more advanced."

Different Body Parts Used Different Embalming Recipes

The chemical signatures also varied depending on which part of the body was sampled.

"For instance, samples from heads often contained different patterns than those from torsos, suggesting embalmers applied distinct recipes to separate parts of the body to possibly aid preservation. This is an area which needs further analysis and research to better understand what techniques were used and why," Dr. Zhao added.

The findings provide a more detailed understanding of known embalming mixtures and offer deeper insight into how these preservation techniques developed over time.

A New Way to Study Mummies Without Damaging Them

Study co-author Richard Evershed, Professor of Chemistry at the University of Bristol, said: "Our volatile analysis proved sensitive enough to detect residues at extremely low concentrations. For example, bitumen biomarkers were previously difficult to detect with earlier soluble residue methods.

"This approach expands the study of ancient Egyptian funerary practices, presenting a clearer, fuller picture of mummification recipes, material choices, and preservation strategies."

The technique could also be valuable for museums and research collections. Sampling the air around mummies provides a fast, non destructive method for examining fragile remains while preserving their physical condition.

Study co-author Ian Bull, Professor of Analytical Chemistry at the University of Bristol, added: "Physical sampling still plays a role for detailed work, yet volatile analysis provides an effective and enlightening first step for studying embalmed remains across collections and time periods."
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant scientific advancement in non-destructive analysis techniques that reveal new insights into ancient Egyptian mummification practices. This breakthrough has broad implications for archaeology, history, and museum conservation, benefiting society's understanding of cultural heritage.
articles.personality_title: Scientists uncover 2,000-year-old Egyptian mummification secrets through mummy scents
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For centuries, people have been curious about how ancient Egyptians preserved their dead through mummification. Many details about the materials and methods they used have remained unknown.

**What happened** – Researchers at the University of Bristol studied the smell around tiny mummy fragments using advanced tools that detect chemicals in the air. Instead of damaging the samples, they captured scents from 35 mummy pieces dating from 3200 BC to 395 AD. They found 81 different chemical compounds linked to fats, oils, resins, beeswax, and bitumen used in embalming.

**Impact** – This study reveals how mummification became more complex over time, with earlier mummies using simpler ingredients and later ones including expensive imported resins and bitumen. The chemical patterns also differ depending on the body part, showing that embalmers used special recipes for different areas. The non-destructive scent analysis offers a new way to study fragile mummies without harming them.

**What's next step** – Scientists plan to explore why different embalming recipes were used on various body parts and apply this scent analysis method to more mummies in museums. This approach could help preserve cultural heritage while learning more about ancient Egyptian rituals.

**One-sentence takeaway** – By analyzing the scents of ancient mummies, scientists have unlocked detailed secrets about how Egyptian embalming evolved over thousands of years without damaging precious artifacts.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques révèlent les secrets de la momification égyptienne vieille de 2 000 ans grâce aux odeurs
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis des siècles, les gens s'interrogent sur la manière dont les anciens Égyptiens préservaient leurs morts par momification. Beaucoup de détails sur les matériaux et méthodes utilisés restent inconnus.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de l'Université de Bristol ont étudié l'odeur autour de minuscules fragments de momies en utilisant des outils avancés pour détecter les produits chimiques dans l'air. Au lieu d'endommager les échantillons, ils ont capturé les odeurs de 35 morceaux de momies datant de 3200 av. J.-C. à 395 apr. J.-C. Ils ont trouvé 81 composés chimiques liés aux graisses, huiles, résines, cire d'abeille et bitume utilisés pour l'embaumement.

**Impact** – Cette étude montre comment la momification est devenue plus complexe avec le temps, les premières momies utilisant des ingrédients simples et les plus récentes des résines importées coûteuses et du bitume. Les motifs chimiques varient aussi selon la partie du corps, indiquant que les embaumeurs utilisaient des recettes spécifiques. L'analyse des odeurs sans destruction offre une nouvelle façon d'étudier les momies fragiles sans les abîmer.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques prévoient d'explorer pourquoi différentes recettes ont été utilisées sur diverses parties du corps et d'appliquer cette méthode d'analyse olfactive à plus de momies dans les musées. Cette approche pourrait aider à préserver le patrimoine culturel tout en approfondissant les connaissances sur les rituels égyptiens anciens.

**Résumé en une phrase** – En analysant les odeurs des momies anciennes, les scientifiques ont découvert des secrets détaillés sur l'évolution de l'embaumement égyptien sur des milliers d'années sans endommager les précieux artefacts.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren secretos de la momificación egipcia de hace 2,000 años a través del olor de las momias
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante siglos, las personas han tenido curiosidad sobre cómo los antiguos egipcios preservaban a sus muertos mediante la momificación. Muchos detalles sobre los materiales y métodos usados seguían siendo desconocidos.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Bristol estudiaron el olor alrededor de pequeños fragmentos de momias usando herramientas avanzadas que detectan químicos en el aire. En lugar de dañar las muestras, capturaron los olores de 35 piezas de momias que datan desde 3200 a.C. hasta 395 d.C. Encontraron 81 compuestos químicos relacionados con grasas, aceites, resinas, cera de abejas y betún usados en el embalsamamiento.

**Impacto** – Este estudio muestra cómo la momificación se volvió más compleja con el tiempo, con momias antiguas usando ingredientes simples y las más recientes incluyendo resinas importadas costosas y betún. Los patrones químicos también varían según la parte del cuerpo, mostrando que los embalsamadores usaban recetas especiales para diferentes áreas. El análisis no destructivo del olor ofrece una nueva forma de estudiar momias frágiles sin dañarlas.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos planean investigar por qué se usaron diferentes recetas en varias partes del cuerpo y aplicar este método de análisis de olores a más momias en museos. Este enfoque podría ayudar a preservar el patrimonio cultural mientras se aprende más sobre los rituales egipcios antiguos.

**Resumen en una frase** – Al analizar los olores de momias antiguas, los científicos han descubierto secretos detallados sobre cómo evolucionó el embalsamamiento egipcio durante miles de años sin dañar los valiosos artefactos.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of an ancient Egyptian mummy wrapped in textured linen bandages, surrounded by softly glowing, delicate wisps of aromatic resin, beeswax, and bitumen vapors rising gently like ethereal ribbons, set against a muted desert background with subtle hieroglyphic patterns blending into the sand.

articles.title: AI and the PhD student: friend or foe?
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 16 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00843-yGraduate students increasingly use artificial-intelligence tools to draft, code and search — but many fear it could erode the very skills a doctorate is meant to build.
articles.content:
Leona Diala uses artificial-intelligence tools for many aspects of her PhD: to search the literature, design presentations, generate code and summarize papers. The tools save her time and give her ideas, she says. “I would say AI is a blessing for researchers today.”

At the same time, Diala, who is studying infectious-disease modelling at the University of Abuja, is concerned that AI overuse is undermining essential academic skills. She checks every reference and fact that AI tools give her and rewrites their text in her own words. But she worries about the next generation of researchers. When she was an undergraduate, she says, “there was no AI like this. We sat for hours and read, practised, tried and retried until we got it. Now people want AI to write everything.” She adds: “AI is a blessing, but it has made students lazy. People don’t go the extra mile to build the skill.”

Leona Diala checks every reference and fact that AI tools give her.Credit: Courtesty of Leona Diala

Diala’s ambivalence is typical of PhD students. When Nature surveyed almost 3,800 PhD students last year, three-quarters thought AI tools could help students to work more efficiently, and 71% felt it was acceptable to use them to support their studies — yet the majority also voiced strong concerns. Some 81% said they don’t fully trust AI tools and 65% worried that AI weakens thinking, research and writing skills.

Since ChatGPT launched in November 2022, AI use has exploded across higher education. In a survey of 1,041 UK undergraduates published in February 2025, 88% admitted to using AI for assessments, up from 53% the year before (see go.nature.com/4d37rcc). The proportion of respondents who had used any AI tool also jumped, from 66% in 2024 to 92% in 2025. Such a rapid change in behaviour is “almost unheard of”, wrote study author Josh Freeman, policy manager at the Higher Education Policy Institute in Oxford, UK, in a statement accompanying the results.

Doctoral students are now charting paths through territory their supervisors never had to navigate. Some use AI daily and swear by it; others refuse to touch it, worried about the cost to their development as researchers. Most fall in between, working out their own rules for when AI helps and when it hinders.

Yinghui He uses a mix of AI tools for tasks such as checking grammar and generating code.Credit: Courtesy of Yinghui He

“AI can be your greatest ally or your worst enemy. It all depends on how you use it,” says Yinghui He, a PhD student at Tsinghua University in Beijing who uses computer methods to study microorganisms in the deep ocean. She turns to AI tools such as ChatGPT and Gemini every day, as well as Chinese AI tools, for tasks including checking grammar in her written English and generating code. “The AI writes code faster than me, and saves me a lot of time,” she says. “But once it’s generated the code, I need to check if it’s right. That is very important.”

That’s a lesson that Richard Ang, a PhD student in soil microbiology at the University of Western Australia in Perth, learnt the hard way. He once asked ChatGPT to calculate fertilizer doses for an experiment. When the experiment failed, he asked the tool to show its thinking. “It totally misunderstood my question,” he says. “AI will never tell us that our design is uncommon, or wrong,” he adds. “If we ask AI to carry out a ridiculous or impossible task, it will do it.” Now, he always asks the tool to explain its reasoning step by step, and he cross-checks answers using multiple tools.

Diala learnt the same lesson when she plotted a graph and asked AI to explain it. “It said a value increased when the graph showed a decrease,” she says. “Before we use AI, we should have some knowledge of what we’re asking, so we can catch mistakes.”

Where to draw the line

The confusion many PhD students feel comes partly from AI’s technological acceleration outpacing the ability of universities to govern it. According to a report published in January by the European University Association in Brussels, which surveyed universities across Europe about their policies for doctoral education, only 5% felt the existing AI guidelines were sufficient (see go.nature.com/4stfddp). The largest group, 38%, reported that they were still establishing policies for the first time, and 13% had no policies at all.

‘Without these tools, I’d be lost’: how generative AI aids in accessibility

In the absence of clear direction, students are turning to educators such as Amina Yonis for answers about how to use these tools responsibly. Yonis (who trained as a molecular biologist at University College London) now runs The Page Doctor, an academic-support company that helps students with writing and editing. She has built a YouTube following of almost 500,000 subscribers by offering practical tips for navigating academic life, including how to use AI ethically in a way that doesn’t harm intellectual development.

“There are parts where I’d say ‘use AI’ and parts where I’d say ‘don’t’,” says Yonis, who is based in Dubai, United Arab Emirates. Tasks such as finding, digesting and organizing research literature fall in the ‘use AI’ column. “It helps you read 50 papers in a month rather than 50 in a year.” But she urges students to avoid the tools for data analysis, to stay in control of the intellectual work and because of data protection: many AI tools do not keep what you tell them confidential.

Amina Yonis offers tips on ethical AI use.Credit: Courtesy of Amina Yonis

Yonis also urges caution in using AI to write a thesis. The technology is useful for coming up with a structure or cleaning up text, she says. But getting it to compose long sections from scratch can be problematic. “If you let AI write and then you look, you’ll struggle to break away from the example,” she says. “It’s better to write it yourself and then use AI to clean up.”

For language help, however, AI can be transformative. Manikandan Palanichamy, an electrical and computer engineer at Østfold University College in Fredrikstad, Norway, who supervises PhD students, says that it gives those who don’t have English as their first language access to cutting-edge research and helps them to express complex ideas more effectively, which “democratizes PhD training significantly”. However, although AI can be used to amplify thinking, it should not replace it, he says. “As a professor, my challenge for PhD or master’s or bachelor’s supervision is teaching students this distinction early.”

Not all AI tools are created equal, Yonis says. She recommends stepping away from generic AI tools and exploring applications that are purpose-built for academics, such as Paperpal for writing or Consensus for literature searches (see ‘AI joins the lab’).

AI joins the lab More AI apps are being purpose-built for research. Most have tiered pricing models, with free limited-use options or free trial periods. We highlight a selection. For discovering and understanding literature, ResearchRabbit maps citation networks and helps to find connected papers. The tool Elicit searches a large body of academic papers and can extract key findings, summarize papers and answer research questions. Consensus returns evidence-based answers with linked citations. Scite looks at how other papers talk about a study — whether they support, contradict or simply mention its findings. Connected Papers, Litmaps and Inciteful (each has a free version) create visual maps of research fields and paper relationships. For data analysis and coding, Julius analyses data sets through language prompts, and generates code and visualizations. Bohrium is a cloud platform for running scientific simulations, notebooks and managing research data, and GitHub Copilot suggests ways to complete your code as you type. For writing and editing, Paperpal offers writing suggestions and language polishing specifically for research papers. The Prism workspace from ChatGPT creator OpenAI, based on the model GPT5.2, lets users refine documents written in LaTeX, a document-preparation system used for some scientific papers. Remember, whichever tools you use, they should help you to learn — not replace your thinking. Never upload sensitive, proprietary or identifiable participant data to AI tools without proper data-use agreements and institutional approval, and don’t rely on AI-generated code without understanding and testing it thoroughly. Don’t use AI to write entire papers or manuscript sections without disclosing what you’ve done — this might violate academic-integrity policies. Finally, if you feel you are becoming too dependent on AI systems, Amina Yonis at academic-support company The Page Doctor has some simple advice: go back to conventional methods. “AI has only been around for about three years. Most people finished PhDs without it. You can do it.”

What skills will matter?

Even with practical guidance on how to use AI responsibly, deeper questions remain. As the technology hurtles forwards, it’s difficult to say precisely what skills PhD students need to hone or protect to navigate the future.

Natalia Bielczyk, a computational neuroscientist who splits her time between California, Poland and the Netherlands and founded Ontology of Value, a company that helps professionals to navigate career transitions, says PhD students might want to think about their value differently in the age of AI. Rather than competing with AI on speed or recall, doctoral students should lean into areas that machines still struggle with: framing good questions, navigating ambiguity and designing ways to test ideas in the real world.

Artificial-intelligence search engines wrangle academic literature

“The superpower of the PhD is that it gives you a systematic approach to solving problems,” she says. AI can help with routine tasks such as scanning the literature or polishing drafts, and so leave more time for the kind of slow, conceptual work that underpins real breakthroughs. “It’s unlikely that AI will replace higher-range thinking any time soon,” Bielczyk suggests. “If anything, it raises the bar for humans to specialize in the parts of research that only humans can do.”
articles.reasoning: The article discusses the positive and responsible use of AI tools by PhD students to enhance research efficiency and accessibility, highlighting practical guidance and ethical considerations. It addresses a significant, broad impact on higher education and research practices, offering substantive insights into how AI can support academic development while preserving critical thinking skills.
articles.personality_title: PhD students balance AI tools with learning essential research skills
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – PhD students are increasingly using artificial intelligence (AI) tools to help with research tasks like writing, coding, and searching academic papers. These tools have become popular since the release of ChatGPT in late 2022, changing how students work in higher education.

**What happened** – Many doctoral students, like Leona Diala from the University of Abuja and Yinghui He from Tsinghua University, use AI daily to save time and improve their work. However, they also check AI-generated information carefully to avoid mistakes. Surveys show most PhD students find AI helpful but worry it may weaken important skills like critical thinking and writing. Universities are still figuring out how to guide students on responsible AI use.

**Impact** – AI tools help students handle large amounts of research faster and assist those who struggle with language barriers. At the same time, students learn to balance using AI with developing their own understanding. Experts suggest AI should support, not replace, human thinking. This balance is important to keep research honest and meaningful.

**What's next step** – Universities are working on clearer rules about AI use in research. Students and educators are exploring which tasks AI should assist with and which require human judgment. New AI programs designed specifically for academics are emerging to help with writing, coding, and literature searches. Continued learning about AI’s strengths and limits will shape the future of doctoral education.

**One-sentence takeaway** – PhD students are learning to use AI tools responsibly to improve research efficiency while maintaining essential skills and critical thinking.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les doctorants apprennent à utiliser l’IA tout en préservant leurs compétences essentielles
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les doctorants utilisent de plus en plus les outils d’intelligence artificielle (IA) pour les aider dans des tâches de recherche comme la rédaction, la programmation et la recherche d’articles. Ces outils sont devenus populaires depuis la sortie de ChatGPT fin 2022, transformant la manière dont les étudiants travaillent dans l’enseignement supérieur.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – De nombreux doctorants, comme Leona Diala de l’Université d’Abuja et Yinghui He de l’Université Tsinghua, utilisent l’IA tous les jours pour gagner du temps et améliorer leur travail. Cependant, ils vérifient attentivement les informations générées par l’IA pour éviter les erreurs. Des enquêtes montrent que la plupart des doctorants trouvent l’IA utile mais craignent qu’elle n’affaiblisse des compétences importantes comme la réflexion critique et la rédaction. Les universités cherchent encore à encadrer l’utilisation responsable de l’IA.

**Impact** – Les outils d’IA aident les étudiants à traiter rapidement de grandes quantités de recherches et soutiennent ceux qui ont des difficultés avec la langue. En même temps, les étudiants apprennent à équilibrer l’usage de l’IA avec le développement de leur propre compréhension. Les experts recommandent que l’IA soutienne, sans remplacer, la pensée humaine. Cet équilibre est essentiel pour garantir une recherche honnête et pertinente.

**Prochaine étape** – Les universités travaillent à des règles plus claires concernant l’usage de l’IA dans la recherche. Étudiants et enseignants explorent les tâches où l’IA doit aider et celles qui demandent un jugement humain. De nouveaux programmes d’IA spécialement conçus pour les chercheurs apparaissent pour aider à la rédaction, à la programmation et à la recherche bibliographique. La compréhension continue des forces et limites de l’IA façonnera l’avenir de la formation doctorale.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les doctorants apprennent à utiliser l’IA de manière responsable pour améliorer l’efficacité de la recherche tout en préservant leurs compétences essentielles et leur esprit critique.
articles.personality_title_es: Los doctorandos aprenden a usar la IA sin perder habilidades esenciales
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los estudiantes de doctorado usan cada vez más herramientas de inteligencia artificial (IA) para ayudar con tareas de investigación como redactar, programar y buscar artículos académicos. Estas herramientas se popularizaron desde el lanzamiento de ChatGPT a finales de 2022, cambiando la forma en que trabajan en la educación superior.

**Qué pasó** – Muchos estudiantes, como Leona Diala de la Universidad de Abuja y Yinghui He de la Universidad Tsinghua, usan IA a diario para ahorrar tiempo y mejorar su trabajo. Sin embargo, revisan cuidadosamente la información generada por la IA para evitar errores. Encuestas muestran que la mayoría encuentra la IA útil, pero teme que pueda debilitar habilidades importantes como el pensamiento crítico y la escritura. Las universidades aún están creando reglas para guiar el uso responsable de la IA.

**Impacto** – Las herramientas de IA ayudan a los estudiantes a manejar grandes cantidades de investigación más rápido y apoyan a quienes tienen dificultades con el idioma. Al mismo tiempo, aprenden a equilibrar el uso de la IA con el desarrollo de su propio entendimiento. Los expertos recomiendan que la IA apoye, pero no reemplace, el pensamiento humano. Este equilibrio es clave para mantener la investigación honesta y valiosa.

**Próximo paso** – Las universidades trabajan en reglas más claras sobre el uso de IA en la investigación. Estudiantes y docentes exploran para qué tareas la IA debe ayudar y cuáles requieren juicio humano. Surgen nuevas aplicaciones de IA diseñadas para académicos que ayudan en redacción, programación y búsqueda bibliográfica. Aprender sobre las fortalezas y límites de la IA definirá el futuro de la formación doctoral.

**Resumen en una frase** – Los doctorandos aprenden a usar herramientas de IA responsablemente para mejorar la eficiencia en la investigación mientras mantienen habilidades esenciales y pensamiento crítico.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of an open book glowing softly on a wooden desk, surrounded by floating, translucent digital code streams and gently swirling paper pages, symbolizing the blend of traditional research and AI assistance in a thoughtful academic journey.
